rules.pattern: Czech stress always falls on the first syllable of a word
rules.explanation: In Czech, the stress always falls on the first syllable of every word. This is a fixed rule — unlike English, there are no exceptions. Examples: PRA-ha, KA-va, MLA-tim. Long vowels are pronounced longer but do NOT receive extra stress.

rules.pattern: ř is a unique Czech sound — a rolled r with ž/š quality
rules.explanation: The letter ř (r with háček) has no equivalent in other languages. It is a raised alveolar non-sonorant trill. To approximate: try saying 'r' and 'zh' simultaneously. It appears in common words like řeka (river), tři (three), Dvořák. Practice slowly — it takes time to master.

rules.rule_name: The letter ě and consonant softening
rules.pattern: ě softens the preceding consonant: dě→ďe, tě→ťe, ně→ně, bě→bje, pě→pje, vě→vje, mě→mě
rules.explanation: When ě follows certain consonants, it softens them. The most common softening: dě sounds like 'dye', tě like 'tye', ně like 'nye'. For bě, pě, vě: insert a 'j' sound (bje, pje, vje). mě is special — it sounds like 'mňe'. This is critical for correct pronunciation and comprehension.

rules.pattern: á/é/í/ó/ú/ů are long versions of a/e/i/o/u — length changes meaning
rules.explanation: Czech distinguishes between short and long vowels. Long vowels are marked with čárka (acute accent: á, é, í, ó, ú) or kroužek (ring: ů). A long vowel is pronounced approximately twice as long as a short one. Importantly, vowel length can change meaning: pivo (beer) vs pívo (a type of beer), sul (he salted) vs sůl (salt). Always pay attention to vowel length.

rules.pattern: č, š, ž, ř, ď, ť, ň — háček modifies consonant pronunciation
rules.explanation: The háček (ˇ) mark changes the sound of consonants: č = ch (as in 'church'), š = sh (as in 'ship'), ž = zh (as in 'measure'), ř = unique Czech sound, ď = softened d, ť = softened t, ň = softened n (like Spanish ñ). These are distinct letters in the Czech alphabet, not modifications.

rules.pattern: Use 'dobrý den' + 'Vy' for formal; 'ahoj' + 'ty' for informal
rules.explanation: Czech has a T-V distinction. Use formal greetings (dobrý den, dobré ráno, dobrý večer) with strangers, elderly people, and in professional settings. Always use 'Vy' (formal you) with these. Use 'ahoj' or 'čau' with friends, family, and people your own age. Use 'ty' (informal you) with these. Using informal with someone who expects formal is considered rude.

rules.pattern: Jmenuju se + name; Jsem z + city; Těší mě
rules.explanation: To introduce yourself in Czech: 'Jmenuju se [name]' (My name is). 'Jsem z [city]' (I'm from). 'Těší mě' (Nice to meet you, literally 'it pleases me'). The formal version of 'my name is' is 'Jmenuji se' (with -i instead of -u), but 'Jmenuju se' is acceptable in most contexts.

rules.pattern: prosím (please/you're welcome), děkuji (thank you), promiňte (excuse me)
rules.explanation: Three essential politeness words: 'prosím' means both 'please' and 'you're welcome'. 'děkuji' (or colloquially 'děkuju') means 'thank you'. 'promiňte' (formal) or 'promiň' (informal) means 'excuse me'. These are used constantly in Czech daily life.

examples.en: Excuse me, where is the station?

rules.pattern: Masculine (animate + inanimate), Feminine, Neuter — identified by ending
rules.explanation: Every Czech noun has a gender, which determines endings for adjectives, pronouns, and verb agreement. You can usually guess the gender from the ending: consonant ending = masculine (hrad, muž, pes), -a ending = feminine (žena, kniha, káva), -o ending = neuter (město, pivo, auto). There are exceptions, but this rule covers ~90% of nouns.

rules.pattern: Masculine is split: animate (people, animals) vs inanimate (objects, concepts)
rules.explanation: Czech distinguishes between masculine animate (living: muž, pes, student, učitel) and masculine inanimate (non-living: hrad, dům, stůl, most). This distinction matters for the accusative case (animate adds -a, inanimate stays the same) and for past tense plural endings. At A1, focus on recognizing which is which.

rules.pattern: Nominative is the dictionary form, used for the subject of sentences
rules.explanation: The nominative case (1. pád) is the base form of a noun — the form you find in dictionaries. It is used when the noun is the subject of the sentence (the one doing the action). Questions answered by nominative: kdo? (who?) and co? (what?). Example: 'Žena pije kávu' — 'žena' is in nominative (she is the subject).

rules.pattern: To je + noun — for identifying and introducing things
rules.explanation: 'To je' (This is / That is) is the most common A1 pattern for identifying objects and people. 'To' is a demonstrative pronoun that doesn't change for gender. Use 'To je' + noun in nominative. For plural: 'To jsou' (These are). Negation: 'To není' (That is not).

rules.pattern: Many professions have male and female forms: -tel → -telka, -ř → -řka
rules.explanation: Czech profession nouns have gendered forms. Common patterns: učitel/učitelka (teacher), student/studentka (student), lékař/lékařka (doctor). The female form typically adds -ka to the male form. Use the form matching the person's gender.

examples.cz: On je učitel. Ona je učitelka.
examples.en: He is a teacher. She is a teacher.

examples.cz: On je student. Ona je studentka.
examples.en: He is a student. She is a student.

examples.cz: On je lékař. Ona je lékařka.
examples.en: He is a doctor. She is a doctor.

rules.pattern: já jsem, ty jsi, on/ona/ono je, my jsme, vy jste, oni/ony jsou
rules.explanation: být is the most important Czech verb. Present tense: jsem (I am), jsi (you are), je (he/she/it is), jsme (we are), jste (you pl. are), jsou (they are). The subject pronoun is usually omitted — 'Jsem student' is more natural than 'Já jsem student'.

rules.pattern: já mám, ty máš, on/ona má, my máme, vy máte, oni/ony mají
rules.explanation: mít is the second essential verb. Present tense: mám (I have), máš (you have), má (he/she has), máme (we have), máte (you pl. have), mají (they have). Used for possession: 'Mám auto' (I have a car). Also used for age: 'Mám dvacet let' (I am twenty years old, lit. 'I have twenty years').

rules.pattern: ne- prefix: nejsem, nejsi, není, nemám, nemáš, nemá
rules.explanation: To negate být and mít, add 'ne-' prefix: nejsem (I am not), nejsi (you are not), není (he/she is not), nejsme (we are not), nejste (you pl. are not), nejsou (they are not). For mít: nemám, nemáš, nemá, nemáme, nemáte, nemají. The negative of 'je' is irregular: 'není' (not 'neje').

rules.rule_name: Question formation with být and mít
rules.pattern: Yes/no questions by intonation; information questions with question words
rules.explanation: Czech yes/no questions are formed by rising intonation — no word order change needed: 'Jsi student?' (Are you a student?). Answers: 'Ano, jsem.' (Yes, I am.) / 'Ne, nejsem.' (No, I'm not.). Information questions use question words: kdo (who), co (what), kde (where), jak (how), odkud (from where). Examples: 'Kde jsi?' (Where are you?), 'Co máš?' (What do you have?), 'Kdo je to?' (Who is that?).

examples.note: lit. 'How many years do you have?'

rules.pattern: Infinitive -át → present: -ám, -áš, -á, -áme, -áte, -ají
rules.explanation: Verbs ending in -át in the infinitive follow this pattern. Example: dělat (to do/make) → dělám, děláš, dělá, děláme, děláte, dělají. Other common -át verbs: znát (to know), čekat (to wait), psát (to write), hrát (to play). Note: some verbs like psát have consonant changes (píšu/píše instead of psám).

rules.pattern: Infinitive -ít → present: -ím, -íš, -í, -íme, -íte, -í
rules.explanation: Verbs ending in -ít follow this pattern. Example: mluvit (to speak) → mluvím, mluvíš, mluví, mluvíme, mluvíte, mluví. All forms except 1st person singular end in -í. Common -ít verbs: prosit (to ask/request), bydlet (to live), učit (to teach), kuřit (to smoke). Note: bydlet conjugates as bydlím, bydlíš, bydlí (adds -l- in present stem).

rules.rule_name: Verb conjugation class 3: -et/-ět and irregular verbs
rules.pattern: -ět verbs: -ím, -íš, -í; irregular: číst → čtu, čteš, čte, čteme, čtete, čtou
rules.explanation: Verbs ending in -ět like rozumět (to understand) → rozumím, rozumíš, rozumí, rozumíme, rozumíte, rozumí. Some verbs are irregular in present tense: číst (to read) → čtu, čteš, čte, čteme, čtete, čtou. jíst (to eat) → jím, jíš, jí, jíme, jíte, jedí. These must be memorized individually.

rules.pattern: Add ne- to the beginning of the verb: nedělám, nemluvím, nečtu
rules.explanation: To negate any Czech verb in present tense, simply add 'ne-' before the verb. Examples: nedělám (I don't do), nemluvíš (you don't speak), nerozumí (he doesn't understand), nečteme (we don't read). Exception: být uses nej- (nejsem, nejsi, není). The negation is consistent and applies to all verbs except být.

rules.pattern: Feminine nouns ending in -a change to -u in accusative: káva → kávu
rules.explanation: The accusative case (4. pád) marks the direct object of a sentence — the thing receiving the action. For feminine nouns ending in -a, change -a to -u: káva → kávu, žena → ženu, kniha → knihu, voda → vodu, škola → školu. The question words for accusative are: koh/ (who as object?) and co? (what as object?).

rules.pattern: Masculine animate nouns change ending in accusative: pes → psa, muž → muže
rules.explanation: Masculine animate nouns (people, animals) change in accusative. Most add -a: pes → psa, student → studenta, učitel → učitele. Some change consonant + add -e: muž → muže, lékař → lékaře. The pattern is: if the noun refers to a living being (masculine), it changes in accusative. This is a key difference from inanimate masculine.

rules.rule_name: Accusative case — masculine inanimate: no change
rules.pattern: Masculine inanimate nouns stay the same in accusative: hrad → hrad, dům → dům
rules.explanation: Masculine inanimate nouns (objects, concepts) do NOT change in accusative. The accusative form is identical to the nominative. Examples: hrad → hrad, dům → dům, stůl → stůl, most → most. This is the easiest accusative rule — if it's a masculine object, it stays the same.

examples.note: hrad = same in nom. and acc.

rules.pattern: Neuter nouns stay the same in accusative: město → město, pivo → pivo, auto → auto
rules.explanation: Neuter nouns (ending in -o, -í) do NOT change in accusative. The accusative form is identical to the nominative. Examples: město → město, pivo → pivo, auto → auto, okno → okno. Combined with masc. inanimate, this means only feminine -a nouns and masculine animate nouns change in accusative.

rules.pattern: já (I), ty (you), on (he), ona (she), ono (it), my (we), vy (you pl.), oni/ony (they)
rules.explanation: Czech personal pronouns: já (I), ty (you sg. informal), on (he), ona (she), ono (it), my (we), vy (you pl./formal), oni (they animate), ony (they inanimate/fem.). 'Oni' is used for masculine animate groups; 'ony' for feminine and neuter groups. Subject pronouns are frequently omitted — the verb ending tells you the person. Use 'vy' for both plural 'you' and formal singular 'you'.

examples.cz: On je učitel. Ona je učitelka.
examples.en: He is a teacher. She is a teacher.

rules.pattern: můj (my), tvůj (your), jeho (his), její (her), náš (our), váš (your pl.), jejich (their)
rules.explanation: Possessive adjectives in Czech: můj (my), tvůj (your sg.), jeho (his), její (her), naše (our), vaše (your pl.), jejich (their). 'jeho' and 'její' and 'jejich' do NOT change for gender. 'můj' and 'tvůj' have special forms: můj (masc), moje (fem/neut); tvůj (masc), tvoje (fem/neut). 'náš' and 'váš' follow: náš/váš (masc), naše/vaše (fem/neut).

rules.pattern: můj/tvůj → moje for feminine and neuter; náš/váš → naše/vaše for feminine and neuter
rules.explanation: Possessive adjectives must agree with the gender of the noun they modify. For můj and tvůj: use můj/tvůj with masculine nouns, moje/tvoje with feminine and neuter nouns. For náš and váš: use náš/váš with masculine, naše/vaše with feminine and neuter. His (jeho), her (její), their (jejich) never change. Examples: můj pes (masc), moje kniha (fem), moje auto (neut), náš dům (masc), naše matka (fem), naše město (neut).

examples.cz: můj bratr, moje sestra, moje auto
examples.en: my brother, my sister, my car

examples.cz: tvůj pes, tvoje kniha, tvoje pivo
examples.en: your dog, your book, your beer

examples.cz: náš dům, naše škola, naše město
examples.en: our house, our school, our city

examples.cz: jeho otec, její matka, jejich auto
examples.en: his father, her mother, their car

rules.rule_name: Pronoun dropping — subject pronouns are optional
rules.pattern: Czech is a pro-drop language — verb endings indicate person, so pronouns are optional
rules.explanation: In Czech, subject pronouns (já, ty, on, ona, my, vy, oni) can almost always be omitted because the verb ending clearly indicates the person. 'Jsem student' is more natural than 'Já jsem student'. Pronouns are used for emphasis or contrast: 'Já jsem z Prahy, ale on je z Brna' (I am from Prague, but he is from Brno). In questions, the pronoun is almost never used: 'Jsi Čech?' (Are you Czech?).

examples.cz: Já jsem z Prahy, on je z Brna.
examples.en: I'm from Prague, he's from Brno.

rules.pattern: rodina (family), matka/máma (mother), otec/táta (father), bratr (brother), sestra (sister)
rules.explanation: Core family vocabulary: rodina (family, fem.), matka or máma (mother, fem.), otec or táta (father, masc. anim.), bratr (brother, masc. anim.), sestra (sister, fem.), syn (son, masc. anim.), dcera (daughter, fem.), babička (grandmother, fem.), dědeček (grandfather, masc. anim.). Note: máma and táta are informal/colloquial, matka and otec are formal.

examples.en: I have a brother and a sister.

rules.pattern: Adjectives must match noun gender: masc -ý, fem -á, neuter -é
rules.explanation: Czech adjectives change their ending to match the gender of the noun they describe. Masculine: -ý (dobrý pes, velký dům). Feminine: -á (dobrá kniha, velká škola). Neuter: -é (dobré pivo, velké město). This applies to all adjectives: nový/nová/nové (new), starý/stará/staré (old), malý/malá/malé (small), hezký/hezká/hezké (pretty). The adjective always comes before the noun.

examples.cz: nové auto, nová škola, nový dům
examples.en: new car, new school, new house

rules.pattern: Hard adjective endings: masc -ý, fem -á, neuter -é (in nominative singular)
rules.explanation: The basic hard adjective endings in nominative singular are: masculine -ý (dobrý, velký, malý, starý, nový, hezký), feminine -á (dobrá, velká, malá, stará, nová, hezká), neuter -é (dobré, velké, malé, staré, nové, hezké). These are the most common adjective endings. Soft adjectives (ending in -í) don't change: cizí (foreign) stays cizí for all genders.

examples.cz: starý dům, stará škola, staré auto
examples.en: old house, old school, old car

rules.pattern: Standard order: possessive adjective + descriptive adjective + noun
rules.explanation: When combining a possessive with a descriptive adjective, the order is: possessive + adjective + noun. Examples: můj starý dům (my old house), moje nová kniha (my new book), naše velké auto (our big car). The descriptive adjective agrees with the noun gender, not the possessive. 'můj' is masculine, 'moje' is feminine/neuter — this is the possessive form, while the adjective ending is separate.

rules.pattern: jedna, dva, tři, čtyři, pět, šest, sedm, osm, devět, deset, jedenáct, dvanáct, třináct, čtrnáct, patnáct, šestnáct, sedmnáct, osmnáct, devatenáct, dvacet
rules.explanation: Czech numbers 0–20 are irregular and must be memorized. Key patterns: numbers 1 and 2 have gender forms — jeden (masc), jedna (fem), jedno (neut) and dva (masc), dvě (fem/neut). Numbers 11–19 end in -náct (eleven = jedenáct, twelve = dvanáct). Ten is 'deset', twenty is 'dvacet'. Stress always on first syllable: JED-na, DVA, TŘI.

rules.pattern: dvacet jedna (21), třicet (30), čtyřicet (40), padesát (50), šedesát (60), sedmdesát (70), osmdesát (80), devadesát (90), sto (100)
rules.explanation: Numbers 21–99 are formed as 'tens + jednotka': dvacet jedna (21), třicet pět (35), čtyřicet sedm (47). Tens are regular: číslo + -cet/-sát. 100 is 'sto'. When counting objects, use the correct gender form: jeden stůl (1 table, masc), jedna kniha (1 book, fem), jedno auto (1 car, neut). For 2: dva stoly, dvě knihy, dvě auta. For 3–4: tři/čtyři + plural nominative. For 5+: noun in genitive plural (pět stolů).

examples.en: How much does it cost? 100 crowns.

rules.pattern: Je jedna hodina. Jsou dvě/tři/čtyři hodiny. Je pět hodin. V jednu hodinu (at 1 o'clock) vs v pět hodin (at 5).
rules.explanation: For 1:00: 'Je jedna hodina.' For 2:00–4:00: 'Jsou dvě/tři/čtyři hodiny.' For 5:00+: 'Je pět/šest/... hodin.' Quarter past = 'čtvrt na + next hour': 3:15 = 'čtvrt na čtyři'. Half past = 'půl + next hour': 3:30 = 'půl čtvrté'. Quarter to = 'tři čtvrtě na + next hour': 3:45 = 'tři čtvrtě na čtyři'. 'V kolik hodin?' = at what time? Use 'v' + accusative: v jednu, v pět.

examples.en: What time is it? It's one o'clock.
The meeting is at five o'clock.

rules.rule_name: Days of the week, months, and dates
rules.pattern: Pondělí, úterý, středa, čtvrtek, pátek, sobota, neděle. Leden, únor, březen... Dnes je pondělí. V pondělí (on Monday).
rules.explanation: Days in Czech are NOT capitalized: pondělí, úterý, středa, čtvrtek, pátek, sobota, neděle. 'Dnes je...' = today is... 'V pondělí...' = on Monday (preposition 'v' + locative for days except pondělí/úterý/pátek — v pondělí, v úterý, ve středu, ve čtvrtek, v pátek, v sobotu, v neděli). Months: leden, únor, březen, duben, květen, červen, červenec, srpen, září, říjen, listopad, prosinec. For dates: 'Je pátého ledna' (It's January 5th) — genitive form.

examples.en: On Tuesday I have Czech class.

rules.pattern: mýt se (wash oneself), učit se (learn/study), dívat se (watch/look), smát se (laugh), bát se (be afraid)
rules.explanation: Reflexive verbs use 'se' or 'si' as part of the verb. 'Se' is the accusative reflexive particle meaning 'oneself'. The particle changes position based on sentence structure: 'Já se myju' (I wash myself — 2nd position rule), but 'Myju se' (when pronoun dropped). Common se-verbs: mýt se (to wash), učit se (to learn/study), dívat se (to look/watch), smát se (to laugh), bát se (to fear), ptát se (to ask), vstávat (to get up — no se, but some regionally use it). The se particle must appear in the sentence — it's NOT optional like subject pronouns.

examples.en: In the morning I wash with cold water.

rules.pattern: kupovat si (buy for oneself), vařit si (cook for oneself), číst si (read for oneself), přát si (wish)
rules.explanation: 'Si' is the dative reflexive particle, meaning 'for/to oneself'. It implies the action is done for the subject's own benefit. Compare: 'Kupuju chleba' (I buy bread — general) vs 'Kupuju si chleba' (I buy myself bread — for me). Common si-verbs: kupovat si, vařit si, číst si, přát si (to wish), myslet si (to think/have an opinion), sednout si (to sit down). The same 2nd position rule applies: 'Já si kupuju kávu' or 'Kupuju si kávu'.

examples.en: I'm buying myself a new book.
I read a book in the evening.
What do you wish for your birthday?

rules.pattern: já se myju, ty se myješ, on/ona/ono se myje, my se myjeme, vy se myjete, oni se myjou
rules.explanation: Reflexive verbs conjugate exactly like regular verbs, with the reflexive particle se/si moving to 2nd position (after the first stressed word in the clause). Full paradigm for 'učit se' (to study): já se učím, ty se učíš, on se učí, my se učíme, vy se učíte, oni se učí. For negation: the particle stays in 2nd position AND the verb gets ne-: 'Já se neučím' (I don't study). The reflexive particle and verb stay together except when a clitic cluster forms.

examples.cz: Já se myju, ty se myješ, on se myje.
examples.en: I wash, you wash, he washes.

rules.pattern: ráno (morning), dopoledne (late morning), v poledne (at noon), odpoledne (afternoon), večer (evening), v noci (at night), každý den (every day), obvykle (usually), často (often), někdy (sometimes), nikdy (never)
rules.explanation: Czech time adverbs are essential for describing daily routines. Key ones: ráno (in the morning), dopoledne (in the late morning), v poledne (at noon), odpoledne (in the afternoon), večer (in the evening), v noci (at night). Frequency adverbs: vždycky (always), obvykle/normálně (usually), často (often), někdy (sometimes), málokdy (rarely), nikdy (never). The time adverb usually comes first or second in the sentence: 'Ráno vstávám v sedm hodin' or 'Vstávám ráno v sedm hodin'.

examples.en: I get up at six in the morning.
In the evening I watch TV.
I usually have breakfast at seven.
I never drink coffee in the evening.

rules.rule_name: jíst and pít — eating and drinking verbs
rules.pattern: já jím, ty jíš, on jí, my jíme, vy jíte, oni jedí || já piju, ty piješ, on pije, my pijeme, vy pijete, oni pijou
rules.explanation: 'Jíst' (to eat) is irregular: jím, jíš, jí, jíme, jíte, jedí. 'Pít' (to drink) is semi-regular: piju/piji, piješ, pije, pijeme, pijete, pijou/pijí. Both take accusative objects: Jím chleba (I eat bread). Piju vodu (I drink water). The nouns change to accusative: feminine -a→-u (polévka→polévku), masculine animate adds -a (having eaten the animate subject), masculine inanimate stays same (chleba→chleba), neuter stays same (pivo→pivo). 'Mám hlad' (I'm hungry), 'Mám žízeň' (I'm thirsty).

examples.en: What are you eating? I'm eating soup.
I'm hungry! Will you have something?

rules.pattern: chléb (masc inan, acc=chleba), polévka (fem, acc=polévku), máslo (neut, acc=máslo), vejce (neut, acc=vejce), sýr (masc inan, acc=sýr)
rules.explanation: Czech food nouns follow gender rules for accusative. Feminine nouns change -a→-u: polévka→polévku, zmrzlina→zmrzlinu, šunka→šunku. Masculine inanimate: usually no change in accusative: sýr→sýr, chléb→chleba (irregular). Neuter: no change: pivo→pivo, mléko→mléko, máslo→máslo. When ordering: 'Dám si...' + accusative: 'Dám si polévku a pivo.' Also note: koláč, guláš, knedlík, svíčková are very common Czech dishes to know.

examples.en: I'll have goulash and a beer.

examples.cz: K snídani mám chleba s máslem.
examples.en: For breakfast I have bread with butter.

rules.pattern: Dám si... (I'll have...). Chtěl bych... (I would like...). Ještě něco? (Anything else?). Platit! (The bill!). Účet, prosím.
rules.explanation: Key restaurant phrases in Czech: When ordering: 'Dám si...' + accusative (I'll have...) — most common. 'Chtěl bych...' (I would like... — more polite, masculine; 'Chtěla bych...' feminine). Waiter asks: 'Co si dáte?' (What will you have?) or 'Ještě něco?' (Anything else?). To ask for the bill: 'Platit!' (Pay!), 'Účet, prosím' (The bill, please), or 'Můžu zaplatit?' (Can I pay?). 'Platit kartou?' = pay by card? 'Hotově' = in cash. 'Dohromady nebo zvlášť?' = together or separately?

examples.cz: Dám si smažený sýr a hranolky.
examples.en: I'll have fried cheese and fries.
I would like a coffee, please.
The bill! Can I pay by card?

examples.cz: Ještě něco? — Ne, děkuji, to je všechno.
examples.en: Anything else? — No, thank you, that's all.

rules.rule_name: Quantities and partitive genitive with food
rules.pattern: trochu (a little), hodně (a lot), kilo (kilogram), litr (liter), kousek (piece)
rules.explanation: When expressing quantity with food, use the genitive case after quantity words. 'Trochu' + genitive = a little of something: 'trochu mléka' (a little milk), 'trochu soli' (a little salt). 'Hodně' + genitive = a lot: 'hodně práce' (a lot of work), 'hodně jídla' (a lot of food). Measurement units: 'kilo brambor' (a kilo of potatoes), 'litr vína' (a liter of wine). This is an A1 introduction — genitive is covered in full later.

examples.en: Do you have a lot of work?

rules.rule_name: Kolik to stojí? — asking and stating prices
rules.pattern: Kolik to stojí? Stojí to... korun. jedna koruna, dvě/tři/čtyři koruny, pět+ korun
rules.explanation: 'Kolik to stojí?' = How much does it cost? 'Stojí to...' = It costs... Price follows the number rule: 1 koruna, 2-4 koruny, 5+ korun. Czech crown = 'koruna' (Kč). Prices are stated as: 'Stojí to dvacet pět korun' (25 Kč). For asking about specific items: 'Kolik stojí ten svetr?' (How much does that sweater cost?). 'To je drahé' = it's expensive, 'To je levné' = it's cheap.

examples.cz: Kolik to stojí? — Stojí to třicet korun.
examples.en: How much does it cost? — It costs 30 crowns.

rules.pattern: Chci... / Chtěl bych... + accusative. Kupuju... + accusative. Můžu...? (Can I...?)
rules.explanation: When shopping, use these key verbs with accusative objects: 'chci' (I want) + accusative, 'chci koupit' (I want to buy), 'kupuju' (I'm buying), 'hledám' (I'm looking for). More polite: 'Chtěl bych...' (I would like). 'Můžu se podívat?' (Can I look?). 'Můžu si to vyzkoušet?' (Can I try it on?). The seller might say: 'Můžu vám pomoct?' (Can I help you?), 'Jakou velikost?' (What size?), 'Ještě něco?' (Anything else?). 'Vezmu si to' = I'll take it.

examples.en: I'm looking for a new sweater.
I would like to try it on.

examples.cz: Můžu vám pomoct? — Jen se dívám.
examples.en: Can I help you? — I'm just looking.

examples.cz: Vezmu si to. Kolik to stojí?
examples.en: I'll take it. How much does it cost?

rules.pattern: svetr (sweater), kalhoty (pants), košile (shirt), bunda (jacket), boty (shoes), šaty (dress). Colors: červený, modrý, zelený, žlutý, černý, bílý.
rules.explanation: Clothes vocabulary with gender: masculine: svetr (sweater), kabát (coat). Feminine: košile (shirt), bunda (jacket), sukně (skirt). Neuter: tričko (t-shirt). Plural only: kalhoty (pants), boty (shoes), šaty (dress — also singular 'šaty' for men's suits). Colors are adjectives and must agree with the noun: červený svetr (red sweater), červená košile (red shirt), červené tričko (red t-shirt). 'Jakou barvu?' = What color? Sizes: malý/střední/velký (S/M/L).

examples.cz: Mám modrý svetr a černé kalhoty.
examples.en: I have a blue sweater and black pants.
What color do you want? — Red.

rules.pattern: velký→větší, malý→menší, dobrý→lepší, špatný→horší, drahý→dražší, levný→levnější
rules.explanation: Czech comparative adjectives for A1 shopping contexts. Common pairs: drahý→dražší (expensive→more expensive), levný→levnější (cheap→cheaper), velký→větší (big→bigger), malý→menší (small→smaller), dobrý→lepší (good→better), špatný→horší (bad→worse). With 'než' (than): 'Tento svetr je levnější než tamten.' = This sweater is cheaper than that one. Superlative adds nej-: nejlevnější (cheapest), nejlepší (best).

examples.en: Do you have a bigger size?

examples.cz: To je nejdražší restaurace ve městě.
examples.en: That's the most expensive restaurant in town.

rules.pattern: Mám rád/ráda... + accusative. Nemám rád... . Máš rád...?
rules.explanation: 'Mít rád' is the most common way to express liking someone or something in Czech. It conjugates like 'mít': mám rád (masc), mám ráda (fem), máš rád/ráda, má rád/ráda, máme rádi, máte rádi, mají rádi. The object must be in accusative: 'Mám rád čokoládu' (I like chocolate — fem acc). 'Nemám rád...' = I don't like. Question: 'Máš rád kávu?' (Do you like coffee?). For activities, use 'rád + verb': 'Rád čtu' (I like to read).

examples.en: Do you like sports? (to a female)

rules.pattern: Rád/Ráda + conjugated verb. Rád čtu, rád sportuju, ráda vařím. Radši... (prefer).
rules.explanation: To say you like DOING something, use 'rád' (masculine) or 'ráda' (feminine) + the conjugated verb. This is the standard way: 'Rád čtu' (I like to read — male), 'Ráda vařím' (I like to cook — female). 'Radši' = rather/prefer: 'Radši čtu než se dívám na televizi' (I prefer reading to watching TV). For negation: 'Nerad/Nerada + verb' = I don't like doing: 'Nerad běhám' (I don't like running). Question: 'Co rád děláš?' (What do you like to do?).

examples.en: I like to do sports and swim.

examples.cz: Co ráda děláš ve volném čase?
examples.en: What do you like to do in your free time?

rules.pattern: číst (read), sportovat (do sports), plavat (swim), běhat (run), vařit (cook), cestovat (travel), hrát na... (play instrument), dívat se na... (watch)
rules.explanation: Common hobby verbs: číst (to read), sportovat (to do sports), plavat (to swim), běhat (to run), jezdit na kole (to ride a bike), lyžovat (to ski), vařit (to cook), cestovat (to travel), malovat (to paint), zpívat (to sing), tančit (to dance). For musical instruments: 'hrát na + accusative' — 'Hraju na kytaru' (I play guitar), 'Hraju na klavír' (I play piano). For watching: 'dívat se na + accusative' — 'Dívám se na film' (I watch a movie). Free time = 'volný čas'. Hobby = 'koníček'.

examples.en: In my free time I like to read.
Do you like riding a bike?

rules.pattern: vždycky (always), často (often), občas (sometimes), málokdy (rarely), nikdy (never)
rules.explanation: Frequency adverbs are essential for discussing hobbies and routines. vždycky/vždy (always), často (often), obvykle (usually), někdy/občas (sometimes), málokdy (rarely), nikdy (never). These typically come in 2nd position in a sentence (after the first stressed element). With 'nikdy', use a NEGATED verb: 'Nikdy nevařím' (I never cook — literally 'never I don't cook'). This double negation is standard Czech. 'Jak často...?' = How often?

examples.en: I often go to the cinema.
I never exercise in the morning.
How often do you do sports?

rules.rule_name: Kde je...? — asking where things are (locative intro)
rules.pattern: Kde je...? (Where is...?). Je v... / na... (It's in/at...). Tady, tam, nahoře, dole.
rules.explanation: 'Kde je...?' is the basic question for locations. Answers use preposition + locative (or sometimes genitive for static locations at A1 level, but we introduce the locative concept). 'V' + locative = in: 'v Praze' (in Prague), 'v obchodě' (in the shop). 'Na' + locative = at/on: 'na nádraží' (at the station), 'na náměstí' (at the square). Key location words: tady (here), tam (there), vedle (next to), naproti (opposite), blízko (near), daleko (far). Directions: rovně (straight), doleva (left), doprava (right).

examples.cz: Kde je nádraží? — Je tam, vlevo.
examples.en: Where is the station? — It's there, on the left.
Where do you live? — I live in Prague.

rules.rule_name: Kam? — motion with accusative vs location with locative
rules.pattern: Kam jdeš? (Where are you going?) vs Kde jsi? (Where are you?) — Jdu do školy vs Jsem ve škole.
rules.explanation: Czech distinguishes MOTION (where to?) from LOCATION (where?). 'Kam?' = where to? — uses prepositions with the accusative case: 'do' + genitive (do školy, do kina, do práce), 'na' + accusative (na náměstí, na nádraží, na oběd). 'Kde?' = where? — uses prepositions with the locative: 'v/ve' + locative (ve škole, v kině, v práci), 'na' + locative (na náměstí, na nádraží). This is the most important distinction for A1 — Czech learners must master this contrast.

examples.cz: Jdu do školy. vs Jsem ve škole.
examples.en: I'm going to school. vs I'm at school.

examples.cz: Jedu na nádraží. vs Jsem na nádraží.
examples.en: I'm going to the station. vs I'm at the station.
Where are you going? — I'm going to work.

rules.rule_name: Transport and movement verbs — jít vs jet
rules.pattern: jít (go on foot), jet (go by vehicle), chodit (walk regularly), jezdit (travel regularly)
rules.explanation: Czech distinguishes walking from riding/driving. 'Jít' = to go on foot, walk. Conjugation: jdu, jdeš, jde, jdeme, jdete, jdou. 'Jet' = to go by vehicle. Conjugation: jedu, jedeš, jede, jedeme, jedete, jedou. For habitual actions: 'chodit' (walk regularly) and 'jezdit' (travel regularly). Transport: autobusem (by bus), tramvají (by tram), metrem (by metro), autem (by car), na kole (by bike), pěšky (on foot). 'Jak se dostanu...?' = How do I get to...?

examples.en: We go by bus to the center.
I go to work by metro.
How do I get to the Castle?

rules.pattern: obchod (shop), restaurace, nádraží (station), nemocnice (hospital), škola, pošta (post office), banka, lékárna (pharmacy), kino, divadlo (theater)
rules.explanation: Key town locations and their gender: Masculine: obchod (shop), park (park), most (bridge). Feminine: restaurace, nemocnice (hospital), škola, banka, lékárna (pharmacy), pošta (post office), ulice (street). Neuter: nádraží (station), náměstí (square), kino (cinema), divadlo (theater), letiště (airport). When giving directions, use: 'Je to na rohu' (It's on the corner), 'Je to vedle banky' (It's next to the bank — genitive after vedle), 'Je to naproti kinu' (It's opposite the cinema — dative after naproti).

examples.en: The restaurant is opposite the cinema.
The post office is next to the bank.

examples.cz: Jděte rovně, pak první ulice doprava.
examples.en: Go straight, then the first street on the right.

rules.pattern: Je... (It is...). Prší. Sněží. Svítí slunce. Je zima/teplo/horko/chladno.
rules.explanation: Weather expressions in Czech use 'Je' (it is) + adjective/adverb: 'Je hezky' (it's nice), 'Je ošklivo' (it's ugly/bad weather), 'Je zima' (it's cold), 'Je teplo' (it's warm), 'Je horko' (it's hot), 'Je chladno' (it's cool/chilly). Verbs: 'Prší' (it's raining), 'Sněží' (it's snowing), 'Svítí slunce' (the sun is shining), 'Fouká vítr' (the wind is blowing). 'Jaké je počasí?' = What's the weather like? 'Předpověď počasí' = weather forecast. Note: 'zima' can also mean 'winter' — context distinguishes them.

examples.cz: Jaké je dnes počasí? — Je hezky.
examples.en: What's the weather like today? — It's nice.

examples.cz: V zimě je zima a sněží.
examples.en: In winter it's cold and it snows.

rules.pattern: jaro (spring), léto (summer), podzim (autumn), zima (winter). Na jaře, v létě, na podzim, v zimě.
rules.explanation: The four seasons in Czech: jaro (spring, neuter), léto (summer, neuter), podzim (autumn, masculine), zima (winter, feminine). For 'in' + season: 'na jaře' (in spring), 'v létě' (in summer), 'na podzim' (in autumn), 'v zimě' (in winter). Note: jaro uses 'na' + locative (na jaře), not 'v'. Léto changes to 'v létě' (locative). Months per season: jaro = březen/duben/květen, léto = červen/červenec/srpen, podzim = září/říjen/listopad, zima = prosinec/leden/únor.

examples.en: In summer we go to the sea.
In winter I ski in the mountains.

rules.pattern: budu, budeš, bude, budeme, budete, budou + infinitive
rules.explanation: Czech forms the future tense of imperfective verbs with 'být' (to be) in future + infinitive. This is the simplest way for A1 learners to express future: budu dělat (I will do), budeš číst (you will read), budeme cestovat (we will travel). Perfective verbs (not covered at A1) form future differently. The future of 'být' itself: budu, budeš, bude, budeme, budete, budou. Common futures for travel: 'Budu v Praze' (I will be in Prague), 'Pojedu do Brna' (I will go to Brno — 'jet' is perfective-like in future: pojedu, pojedeš...).

examples.en: Tomorrow I will be at work.
What will you do on the weekend?
Next week I will go to Prague.

rules.rule_name: Past tense intro — byl, měl, dělal
rules.pattern: Byl jsem, byl jsi, byl... Měl jsem... Dělal jsem... The -l participle + být auxiliary.
rules.explanation: Czech past tense uses the -l participle + auxiliary 'být' in present tense (jsem, jsi, jsme, jste) — except 3rd person which drops the auxiliary. Pattern: já jsem dělal/dělala (masc/fem), ty jsi dělal/dělala, on dělal (no auxiliary), ona dělala, ono dělalo, my jsme dělali/dělaly, vy jste dělali/dělaly, oni dělali, ony dělaly. For the verb 'být': byl jsem, byla jsem. Key travel past phrases: 'Byl jsem v Praze' (I was in Prague), 'Měl jsem se dobře' (I had a good time). This is just an introduction — full past tense mastery comes in A2.

examples.cz: Byl jsem v Praze minulý týden.
examples.en: I was in Prague last week. (male)
I was at the cinema yesterday. (female)

examples.cz: Měl jsem se dobře na dovolené.
examples.en: I had a good time on vacation.
What did you do on the weekend?

rules.pattern: Masculine animate: -a (bratr→bratra, učitel→učitele). Masculine inanimate: -u/-e (dům→domu, čaj→čaje).
rules.explanation: Genitive masculine: animate nouns add -a (bratr→bratra, pán→pána) or -e for soft stems (učitel→učitele, muž→muže). Inanimate typically add -u (dům→domu, most→mostu) but some add -e (čaj→čaje, pokoj→pokoje). The genitive expresses possession (dům bratra = brother's house), origin (z Prahy), and follows certain prepositions (bez, do, od, z/ze).

rules.pattern: Feminine: -y/-i (žena→ženy, ulice→ulice). Neuter: -a/-e (město→města, moře→moře).
rules.explanation: Feminine genitive: -a stems change to -y (žena→ženy, škola→školy), soft stems stay as -e/-i (ulice→ulice, restaurace→restaurace). Neuter genitive: hard stems change -o to -a (město→města, auto→auta), soft stems change to -e (moře→moře, nádraží→nádraží). These are used identically to masculine genitive for possession, prepositions, and quantities.

rules.pattern: With numbers 5+: pět mužů, šest žen, deset měst. Quantity words (hodně, málo, trochu) + genitive.
rules.explanation: After numbers 5 and above, use genitive plural: pět studentů (5 students), deset korun (10 crowns). Quantity expressions: hodně + gen (a lot of), málo + gen (little), trochu + gen (a bit), kolik + gen (how many), několik + gen (several), kilo + gen (a kilo of), litr + gen (a liter of). Genitive plural endings: masc -ů (pánů, hradů), fem -ø (žen, škol), neut -ø (měst, aut). This is an A2-level topic — focus on recognizing and using common patterns.

examples.en: I have a lot of work.
Five students are in the classroom.

rules.pattern: Accusative objects become genitive when negated: Mám čas → Nemám času. Vidím knihu → Nevidím knihy.
rules.explanation: In Czech, when a verb that normally takes an accusative object is negated, the object often shifts to the genitive case. This is especially common with 'mít' (to have): Mám peníze → Nemám peněz, Mám čas → Nemám času. With other verbs: Vidím problém → Nevidím problému. This genitive of negation is a distinctive feature of Czech and other Slavic languages. It's more common in formal/written Czech; in spoken Czech, accusative is often retained.

rules.pattern: Masc -ovi/-u: bratr→bratrovi, pán→pánovi. Fem -e/-i: žena→ženě, ulice→ulici. Neut -u/-i: město→městu, nádraží→nádraží.
rules.explanation: Dative marks the indirect object (to whom? for whom?). Dávám knihu bratrovi (I give a book TO my brother). Masculine animate: typically -ovi (bratrovi, pánovi) or -i for soft stems (muži, učiteli). Masculine inanimate: -u (domu, mostu). Feminine: -e for hard stems (ženě, škole), -i for soft stems (ulici, restauraci). Neuter: -u for hard stems (městu, autu), -i for soft stems (nádraží, moři). Plural dative: -ům for masc/neut, -ám for fem.

examples.en: I give a gift to mom.
I'm writing an email to the teacher.

rules.pattern: Pomáhat + dat, rozumět + dat, telefonovat + dat, věřit + dat, radit + dat.
rules.explanation: Several common Czech verbs require the dative case, even though their English equivalents use direct objects. Key verbs: pomáhat (to help — pomáhám matce), rozumět (to understand — rozumím češtině), telefonovat (to telephone — telefonuju kamarádovi), věřit (to believe — věřím ti), radit (to advise — radím studentovi), volat (to call — volám doktorovi), odpovídat (to answer — odpovídám učiteli). This is a major source of errors for learners because English doesn't mark dative.

examples.en: I call my mother every day.

rules.rule_name: Líbit se and chutnat — liking in dative
rules.pattern: Líbí se mi... (I like...), Chutná mi... (I like the taste of...). The liked thing is the subject (nominative), the person is dative.
rules.explanation: Czech expresses 'liking' differently from English. With 'líbit se': the thing liked is the subject (nominative) and the person who likes it is in dative. 'Líbí se mi Praha' = literally 'Prague pleases itself to me' = I like Prague. Same with 'chutnat': 'Chutná mi pivo' = I like the taste of beer. Note: the thing liked determines the verb form (3rd sg or pl): Líbí se mi ten film (sg), Líbí se mi ty filmy (pl). This is fundamentally different from 'mít rád' (which uses accusative).

examples.en: I like the taste of Czech beer.

rules.pattern: Je mi zima/teplo/dobře/špatně. Je mi 20 let. Jde mi to. Záleží mi na tom.
rules.explanation: Czech uses dative for many impersonal expressions describing physical or emotional states. Je mi zima (I'm cold — literally 'it is cold to me'), Je mi teplo (I'm warm), Je mi dobře (I feel good), Je mi špatně (I feel sick/bad), Je mi smutno (I feel sad), Je mi líto (I'm sorry). Age is also dative: Je mi 20 let (I'm 20 years old). Jde mi to (I'm good at it / it works for me). Tyto dative expressions are extremely common in everyday Czech.

examples.cz: Je mi zima. Můžu zavřít okno?
examples.en: I'm cold. Can I close the window?

examples.cz: Jak ti je? — Je mi dobře.
examples.en: How are you? — I feel good.

rules.pattern: v + loc: v Praze, v pokoji, v kině. na + loc: na náměstí, na nádraží, na univerzitě.
rules.explanation: The locative case is used ONLY after prepositions — never alone. Primary prepositions: v/ve (in) and na (on/at). V pokoji (in the room), v Praze (in Prague), na stole (on the table), na univerzitě (at the university). Also used with o (about): Mluvím o práci (I talk about work), Kniha o Praze (a book about Prague). With po (after/along): po obědě (after lunch), po ulici (along the street). With při (during/at): při práci (while working). Locative endings are similar to dative for many nouns.

examples.en: The book is on the table.

rules.pattern: s kým/čím? (with whom/what?). Means: autem, vlakem, metrem. Professions: pracovat jako... (+ nom or instr). Time: na jaře → na jaře, ale v zimě → zimou.
rules.explanation: Instrumental expresses the means or instrument: jedu autem (I go by car), píšu perem (I write with a pen). Accompaniment: s + instrumental: Jdu s kamarádem (I go with a friend). Masculine ending: -em (bratr→bratrem, stroj→strojem). Feminine: -ou (žena→ženou, ulice→ulicí). Neuter: -em (město→městem, moře→mořem). The instrumental is also used with být for temporary states: Byl jsem učitelem (I was a teacher). With zájem o: Mám zájem o studium.

examples.en: I go to work by car.
I'm going to the cinema with a friend.

rules.pattern: o kom/čem? Mluvit o..., přemýšlet o..., kniha o..., film o..., starat se o...
rules.explanation: The preposition 'o' + locative means 'about' and is one of the most common uses of the locative case. Key verbs and phrases: mluvit o (talk about), přemýšlet o (think about), číst o (read about), psát o (write about), snít o (dream about), starat se o (take care of — note: o + accusative for most meanings of 'starat se'). Also: kniha o Praze (a book about Prague), film o lásce (a film about love), článek o politice (an article about politics). This is essential for A2 conversational Czech.

examples.cz: O čem mluvíte? — Mluvíme o dovolené.
examples.en: What are you talking about? — We're talking about vacation.

examples.cz: Četl jsem knihu o historii Prahy.
examples.en: I read a book about the history of Prague.

rules.pattern: Plural instrumental: -y/-i/-ami (muži, ženami, městy). Time: na jaře, v létě, na podzim, v zimě (loc). But: před rokem (instr).
rules.explanation: Instrumental plural: masculine -y/-i (muži, studenty, hrady, stroji), feminine -ami (ženami, školami, ulicemi), neuter -y/-i (městy, auty, moři). This is particularly important for accompaniment: Jdu s přáteli do kina (I go with friends to the cinema). Time expressions: před + instrumental = ago (před rokem = a year ago, před týdnem = a week ago). Mezi + instrumental = between (mezi domy = between houses). Nad + instrumental = above, Pod + instrumental = under.

examples.cz: Před rokem jsem byl v Praze.
examples.en: A year ago I was in Prague.

rules.rule_name: Past tense — formation and gender agreement
rules.pattern: -l (masc), -la (fem), -lo (neut), -li (masc pl), -ly (fem/neut pl). Auxiliary: jsem/jsi/jsme/jste (3rd person omitted).
rules.explanation: Czech past tense = -l participle + být auxiliary (present tense). Já jsem dělal/dělala, ty jsi dělal/dělala, on dělal, ona dělala, ono dělalo, my jsme dělali/dělaly, vy jste dělali/dělaly, oni dělali, ony dělaly, ona dělala. The 3rd person drops the auxiliary (on dělal, NOT on je dělal). Gender matters: masculine uses -l, feminine -la, neuter -lo. Plural: at least one male → -li, otherwise -ly. This is a cornerstone of A2 Czech.

examples.en: Yesterday I was at the cinema. (male)

rules.pattern: jít→šel/šla/šlo/šli/šly. jet→jel/jela/jelo/jeli/jely. The auxiliary follows the -l participle: šel jsem, šla jsi.
rules.explanation: Motion verbs jít and jet have irregular past participles: jít → šel (masc), šla (fem), šlo (neut), šli (masc pl), šly (fem/neut pl). Jet → jel/jela/jelo/jeli/jely. Also: jíst → jedl/jedla (to eat). Číst → četl/četla (to read). Vézt → vezl/vezla (to transport). Moci → mohl/mohla (could). The auxiliary word order can be: 'Šel jsem do kina' or 'Já jsem šel do kina'.

examples.cz: Kam jsi šel včera? — Šel jsem do práce.
examples.en: Where did you go yesterday? — I went to work.
Did you go by train? (fem) — Yes, I did.

rules.pattern: Imperfective: dělat, psát, číst, kupovat (process/ongoing). Perfective: udělat, napsat, přečíst, koupit (completed action).
rules.explanation: Czech verbs come in aspect pairs. Imperfective = ongoing, repeated, or process-focused (dělám = I am doing / I do). Perfective = completed, one-time result (udělám = I will do / I get it done). This is the most difficult concept in Czech for English speakers. Common pairs: psát/napsat (write), číst/přečíst (read), dělat/udělat (do/make), kupovat/koupit (buy), jíst/sníst (eat), vařit/uvařit (cook), učit se/naučit se (learn), vidět/uvidět (see). Perfective can NEVER be used in present tense for present meaning — it always expresses future.

examples.en: I was writing the letter all afternoon. (imperfective, process)
I wrote the letter in an hour. (perfective, completed)
Have you read that book yet? (perfective, result)

rules.pattern: včera (yesterday), předevčírem (day before yesterday), minulý týden/měsíc/rok (last week/month/year), před rokem (a year ago).
rules.explanation: Key time markers for past tense narratives: včera (yesterday), předevčírem (the day before yesterday), minulý týden (last week), minulý měsíc (last month), minulý rok (last year), před + instrumental (ago): před hodinou (an hour ago), před rokem (a year ago). Také: onehdy (the other day), kdysi (once/formerly), dříve (earlier/previously). These time expressions typically appear at the beginning or in 2nd position of sentences and trigger past tense.

examples.en: Yesterday I met an old friend.

examples.cz: Minulý týden jsme byli v Brně.
examples.en: Last week we were in Brno.

examples.cz: Před rokem jsem začal studovat češtinu.
examples.en: A year ago I started studying Czech.

rules.pattern: budu/budeš/bude/budeme/budete/budou + infinitive. Budu pracovat, budeš číst, budeme cestovat.
rules.explanation: Imperfective (ongoing/repeated) future = budu + infinitive. This is used for actions that will be ongoing, repeated, or descriptive in the future: Budu se učit česky (I will be learning Czech), Zítra budu celý den pracovat (Tomorrow I'll work all day). Unlike perfective future (which is simple), this form emphasizes the process or duration. Common time markers: zítra (tomorrow), příští týden (next week), za chvíli (in a moment), brzy (soon). Note: být itself uses budu/budeš... directly (I will be = budu).

examples.en: Tomorrow I will work all day.

examples.cz: Příští rok budeme cestovat po Evropě.
examples.en: Next year we will travel around Europe.

rules.rule_name: Perfective future — present form = future meaning
rules.pattern: napíšu, přečtu, udělám, koupím — perfective verbs in present conjugation express FUTURE.
rules.explanation: This is the key difference from imperfective: perfective verbs conjugated in present form express future meaning, NOT present. Napíšu dopis = I will write a letter (future, not 'I am writing'). Udělám to = I'll do it (future). This is why Czech has no separate future conjugation — perfective verbs use their present form for future. Compare: Píšu dopis (I am writing a letter — imperfective, present) vs Napíšu dopis (I will write a letter — perfective, future). This dual function of present-form perfectives is fundamental to Czech.

examples.en: I'll write you an email tomorrow.
Will you buy me a coffee?

rules.pattern: Já bych, ty bys, on/ona/ono by, my bychom, vy byste, oni by. + past participle (-l/-la/-lo).
rules.explanation: Conditional = bych/bys/by + -l participle. Chtěl bych (I would like), Měl bys (you should), Mohli bychom (we could). The conditional expresses wishes, polite requests, hypothetical situations: Chtěl bych kávu (I'd like a coffee), Měl bys jít k doktorovi (You should go to the doctor), Kdybych měl čas, šel bych do kina (If I had time, I would go to the cinema). For past conditional: byl bych + -l participle: Byl bych přišel (I would have come).

examples.en: I would like a coffee, please. (male)

examples.cz: Kdybych měl peníze, koupil bych si nové auto.
examples.en: If I had money, I would buy a new car.

rules.pattern: Kdybych, kdybys, kdyby, kdybychom, kdybyste, kdyby + -l participle. Word order: kdybych + past participle.
rules.explanation: Kdyby (if) + conditional forms: Kdybych věděl... (If I knew...), Kdybys chtěl... (If you wanted...), Kdyby přišel... (If he came...). The verb in the kdyby-clause is always in past tense: Kdybych měl čas (If I had time — literally 'if I had had time'). The main clause uses bych + past: šel bych (I would go). This is a complex but essential A2 structure for hypothetical situations. The word order: kdybych + past participle + rest of clause.

examples.cz: Kdybych měl čas, šel bych do kina.
examples.en: If I had time, I would go to the cinema.
If you want, we can go together.

examples.cz: Kdyby to věděla, řekla by ti to.
examples.en: If she knew it, she would tell you.

rules.pattern: -ější/-ejší (mladý→mladší, starý→starší, chytrý→chytřejší), -ší (čistý→čistší, bohatý→bohatší), or -í (tichý→tišší).
rules.explanation: Regular comparatives add -(ěj)ší to the adjective stem. Mladý (young) → mladší (younger). Starý (old) → starší (older). Krásný (beautiful) → krásnější. The stem often changes: drahý → dražší (expensive→more expensive), tichý → tišší (quiet→quieter). Use 'než' (than) for comparisons: Praha je větší než Brno (Prague is bigger than Brno). The comparative adjective declines like a soft adjective (mladší muž, mladšího muže, mladší žena...).

examples.cz: Můj bratr je mladší než já.
examples.en: My brother is younger than me.

rules.pattern: dobrý→lepší, špatný→horší, velký→větší, malý→menší, dlouhý→delší, krátký→kratší, vysoký→vyšší, nízký→nižší.
rules.explanation: Several common adjectives have irregular comparatives that must be memorized. The most important: dobrý → lepší (good → better), špatný → horší (bad → worse), velký → větší (big → bigger), malý → menší (small → smaller), dlouhý → delší (long → longer), krátký → kratší (short → shorter), vysoký → vyšší (tall → taller), nízký → nižší (low → lower), drahý → dražší (expensive → more expensive), blízký → bližší (near → nearer). These are high-frequency words essential for everyday conversation.

examples.cz: Jeho byt je větší než můj.
examples.en: His apartment is bigger than mine.

examples.cz: Cítím se dnes hůř než včera.
examples.en: I feel worse today than yesterday.

rules.pattern: nej- + comparative: nejlepší, největší, nejdražší. Declines like a soft adjective.
rules.explanation: Superlative = prefix nej- + comparative form. Nejlepší (best), nejhorší (worst), největší (biggest), nejmenší (smallest), nejdražší (most expensive), nejlevnější (cheapest). The superlative can be used attributively (nejlepší restaurace = the best restaurant) or predicatively (Ta restaurace je nejlepší = That restaurant is the best). With 'z' + genitive for 'of all': nejlepší z restaurací (the best of the restaurants), nejchytřejší ze studentů (the cleverest of the students).

examples.cz: To je nejlepší restaurace ve městě.
examples.en: That's the best restaurant in the city.

examples.cz: On je nejchytřejší student ve třídě.
examples.en: He's the cleverest student in the class.

examples.cz: Tohle je nejdražší auto, jaké jsem kdy viděl.
examples.en: This is the most expensive car I've ever seen.

rules.pattern: rychle→rychleji→nejrychleji, dobře→lépe→nejlépe, špatně→hůře→nejhůře, často→častěji→nejčastěji.
rules.explanation: Adverbs also form comparatives and superlatives. Pattern: adverb → -eji/-ěji (comparative) → nej- (superlative). Rychle (quickly) → rychleji → nejrychleji. Notable irregulars: dobře→lépe (well→better), špatně→hůře (badly→worse), hodně→více (a lot→more), málo→méně (little→less), brzy→dříve (soon→earlier), dlouho→déle (long→longer). These are crucial for expressing 'I speak better now' (Mluvím teď lépe), 'Come earlier' (Přijď dříve).

examples.en: You speak Czech better than me.

examples.cz: Chodím do kina častěji než dřív.
examples.en: I go to the cinema more often than before.

rules.pattern: Vím, že... Myslím, že... Řekl, že... + normal word order after že.
rules.explanation: 'Že' (that) introduces a subordinate clause with normal word order. Vím, že máš pravdu (I know that you're right). Myslím, že přijde zítra (I think that he'll come tomorrow). Řekla, že je nemocná (She said that she is sick). Unlike German/Dutch, Czech does NOT send the verb to the end after 'že'. The subject-verb order stays the same: že + subject + verb + rest. This is simpler than many learners expect. Common verbs: říct/říkat (say), myslet (think), vědět (know), slyšet (hear), vidět (see), doufat (hope).

rules.rule_name: Protože and když — cause and time clauses
rules.pattern: Protože + clause (because). Když + clause (when/whenever). Word order: standard SVO, auxiliary clitics in 2nd position.
rules.explanation: 'Protože' (because) introduces a reason clause: Nepřišel, protože byl nemocný (He didn't come because he was sick). 'Když' (when/whenever) for factual or repeated events: Když prší, zůstávám doma (When it rains, I stay home). Both use standard word order. With 'protože', the se/si reflexive stays near the verb: protože se učím (because I'm studying). With 'když', clitics follow: když se vrátím (when I return). Jestli/zda = if/whether for indirect questions: Nevím, jestli přijde (I don't know if he'll come).

examples.en: He didn't come because he was sick.
When I have time, I like to read.
I don't know if I'll make it.

rules.pattern: Abych, abys, aby, abychom, abyste, aby + -l participle. Aby = 'so that / in order to'.
rules.explanation: 'Aby' introduces purpose clauses — why you do something. It uses the conditional forms + -l participle: Musím se učit, abych udělal zkoušku (I must study so that I pass the exam). Přišel jsem dřív, abychom si mohli popovídat (I came earlier so we could chat). When the subject is the same in both clauses, Czech prefers aby over the infinitive: Chci, abys přišel (I want you to come) vs Chci přijít (I want to come — same subject). The aby form bych/bys/by/bychom/byste/by matches the subject of the subordinate clause.

examples.cz: Učím se česky, abych mohl pracovat v Praze.
examples.en: I'm learning Czech so that I can work in Prague.
I want you to help me.
He closed the window so it wouldn't be cold.

rules.pattern: Main clause: SVO. Subordinate clause after že/když/protože: SVO (normal). After aby/kdyby: S + conditional + -l participle. Clitics (se, si, mi, ti, ho) in 2nd position.
rules.explanation: Czech word order in complex sentences follows consistent rules. In main clauses: standard SVO with clitics in 2nd position (Já se učím česky). After že, protože, když, jestli: normal word order, clitics in 2nd position (Vím, že se učíš česky). After aby/kdyby: the aby/kdyby form takes 2nd position, followed by the -l participle (Chci, aby ses učil). The reflexive se/si is absorbed into the aby form: aby + se → aby ses, aby + si → aby sis. For 1st person: abych se, 2nd: aby ses, 3rd: aby se.

examples.en: I know that you're studying Czech.
I want you to study more.

examples.cz: Když jsem přišel domů, nikdo tam nebyl.
examples.en: When I came home, nobody was there.

rules.pattern: muset + infinitive — expresses strong obligation or necessity
rules.explanation: The modal verb muset expresses internal or external obligation — 'must' or 'have to'. It conjugates: musím, musíš, musí, musíme, musíte, musí. The main verb stays in the infinitive. Unlike English, there is no separate 'have to' — muset covers both 'must' and 'have to'. Negation: nemuset = 'don't have to' (lack of obligation, NOT 'must not').

examples.en: You don't have to come if you don't want to.

rules.pattern: moct + infinitive — expresses ability or permission
rules.explanation: The modal verb moct expresses ability or permission — 'can' or 'be able to'. It conjugates: můžu/mohu, můžeš, může, můžeme, můžete, mohou. The forms můžu and mohu are interchangeable (můžu is more common in speech, mohu in formal writing). Negation: nemoci = 'cannot'. This is the most irregular Czech modal verb.

rules.pattern: smět + infinitive — expresses permission or prohibition
rules.explanation: The modal verb smět expresses permission — 'be allowed to' or 'may'. It conjugates: smím, smíš, smí, smíme, smíte, smí. Negation: nesmět = 'must not' / 'be not allowed to'. This is the KEY difference from muset: nesmět = prohibition (you must NOT), while nemuset = lack of obligation (you don't have to). This distinction is essential for Czech learners.

examples.en: Children are not allowed to drink alcohol.
Are we allowed to smoke here?

rules.pattern: Conjugated modal + infinitive of main verb — modal is conjugated, main verb stays in infinitive
rules.explanation: All Czech modal verbs (muset, moct, smět, chtít) follow the same pattern: the modal verb is conjugated for person and number, while the main verb remains in the infinitive form. The modal verb is always in the second position (if there's a clitic cluster, the modal comes after clitics). Word order: Subject + modal + infinitive + rest. The subject can be dropped when clear from context.

examples.cz: Můžete mi říct, kde je nádraží?
examples.en: Can you tell me where the station is?
We must not forget our documents.

rules.pattern: muset (obligation), moct (ability/permission), smět (formal permission), nesmět (prohibition)
rules.explanation: Czech distinguishes three types of modality that English often conflates: OBLIGATION (muset — you must/have to), ABILITY/PERMISSION (moct — you can/are able to), and FORMAL PERMISSION/PROHIBITION (smět/nesmět — you may/may not). Common confusion: 'nesmíš' means 'you must not' (prohibition), while 'nemusíš' means 'you don't have to' (no obligation). For expressing rules: Je dovoleno (it is allowed), Je zakázáno (it is forbidden), Je nutné (it is necessary), Je povinné (it is mandatory).

examples.en: You must not take photos in the museum. (prohibition)

rules.rule_name: Bolí mě — expressing pain with body parts
rules.pattern: bolí + accusative pronoun (mě, tě, ho, ji, nás, vás, je) + body part (nominative)
rules.explanation: To say 'something hurts' in Czech, use the verb bolit (to hurt/ache) in third person singular 'bolí', followed by the accusative form of the pronoun (mě = me, tě = you, ho = him, ji = her, nás = us, vás = you.pl, je = them), and then the body part in the nominative. The verb bolí is always third person — it doesn't conjugate for the person experiencing pain. For plural body parts (eyes, ears, hands, legs), use bolí + pronoun + plural nominative.

examples.en: My head hurts. / I have a headache.

rules.rule_name: cítit se — feeling unwell and reflexive health verbs
rules.pattern: cítit se + adverb (dobře, špatně, unavený, nemocný)
rules.explanation: The reflexive verb cítit se means 'to feel' (one's health/state). It conjugates: cítím se, cítíš se, cítí se, cítíme se, cítíte se, cítí se. Common combinations: cítit se dobře (feel well), cítit se špatně (feel unwell), cítit se unavený (feel tired), cítit se nemocný (feel sick). Other health reflexives: bolet se (to hurt — rare, usually just bolí), zvracet (to vomit — not reflexive), kašlat (to cough — not reflexive), kýchát (to sneeze — not reflexive). For temperature: mít teplotu (to have a fever).

rules.rule_name: At the doctor — questions and medical phrases
rules.pattern: Common doctor-patient exchanges: Co vás trápí? / Boli mě... / Ukážete jazyk? / Vypijte tohle.
rules.explanation: When visiting a Czech doctor, these phrases are essential. Doctor asks: Co vás trápí? (What's bothering you?), Na co si stěžujete? (What are you complaining about?), Ukážete jazyk? (Show your tongue?), Otevřete ústa (Open your mouth). Patient says: Jdu k lékaři (I'm going to the doctor), Boli mě hlava/krk/břicho (My head/throat/stomach hurts), Mám teplotu (I have a fever), Mám kašel (I have a cough), Mám rýmu (I have a runny nose), Potřebuji recept (I need a prescription), Kde je lékárna? (Where is the pharmacy?). Doctor may say: Vypijte tohle (Drink this), Užívejte tyto tablety (Take these tablets), Přijďte za týden (Come back in a week).

examples.cz: Bolí mě krk a mám teplotu.
examples.en: My throat hurts and I have a fever.
I need a prescription for medicine.
Take these tablets three times a day.

rules.pattern: být (conjugated) + noun in instrumental case — jsem učitelem, je lékařem
rules.explanation: In Czech, professions are often expressed with být + the instrumental case (not nominative). This construction answers 'What do you do?': jsem učitelem (I am a teacher), je lékařem (he is a doctor), je studentem (he is a student), je inženýrkou (she is an engineer). The instrumental endings: masculine -em/-ou, feminine -ou, neuter -em. Note: you can also use the nominative (jsem učitel), which is less formal and more common in everyday speech. The instrumental form emphasizes the profession as a role.

examples.en: He is a doctor at the hospital.
We are students. (nominative — common in speech)

rules.pattern: pracovat jako + nominative noun — Pracuji jako učitel. / Dělá co + accusative
rules.explanation: To describe what you do, two constructions are common: (1) pracovat jako + nominative (Pracuji jako učitel = I work as a teacher) — the noun stays in nominative because 'jako' (as/like) preserves the original form. (2) dělat + accusative (Dělám učitele = I do teaching / I work as a teacher) — the noun takes accusative. Both are natural and interchangeable. For asking about someone's job: Co děláš? (What do you do?) or Čím se živíš? (What do you do for a living?) or Jaké je tvoje povolání? (What is your profession?).

examples.en: What do you do? — I'm a teacher.
What do you do for a living?

rules.pattern: učit se (learn), studovat (study), zkouška (exam), předmět (subject) — common school constructions
rules.explanation: Czech distinguishes učit se (to learn — acquiring knowledge, reflexive) from učit (to teach — without 'se'). Studovat (to study at an institution) is used for university/school enrollment. Common constructions: učit se česky (learn Czech), studovat na univerzitě (study at university), dělat zkoušku (take an exam), udělat zkoušku (pass an exam), propadnout u zkoušky (fail an exam). Subjects: předmět. Academic year: školní rok. Semester: semestr. Grade/mark: známka (1=best, 5=worst in Czech system). To graduate: promoce / promovat.

rules.rule_name: Housing vocabulary — rooms and furniture genders
rules.pattern: Room names by gender: byt (masc inan), kuchyně (fem), ložnice (fem), koupelna (fem); furniture: stůl (masc inan), židle (fem), postel (fem), skříň (fem)
rules.explanation: Czech housing vocabulary follows standard gender rules but has some surprises. Rooms: byt (masc inanimate, consonant ending), pokoj (masc inanimate, consonant), kuchyně (feminine, -e ending), ložnice (feminine, -e ending), koupelna (feminine, -a ending), předsíň (feminine, consonant+soft ending). Furniture: stůl (masc inanimate), židle (feminine), postel (feminine), pohovka (feminine), skříň (feminine), regál (masc inanimate), komoda (feminine). Appliances: lednice (feminine), sporák (masc inanimate), pračka (feminine), televize (feminine). Remembering gender is essential for correct adjective agreement: velký byt, velká kuchyně, velké okno.

examples.en: I have a new apartment. (byt = masc inanimate)
The shelf is in the living room.

rules.pattern: v/ve (in) + locative, na (on) + locative, u (by/at) + genitive — location in rooms
rules.explanation: For describing where things are in a house, use these preposition-case combinations: v/ve + locative (v pokoji = in the room, v kuchyni = in the kitchen, v bytě = in the apartment), na + locative (na stole = on the table, na polici = on the shelf, na posteli = on the bed), u + genitive (u stolu = by the table, u okna = by the window, u dveří = by the door). Note the locative endings: masculine -u/-ovi, feminine -e/-i, neuter -o/-e. Also: před (in front of) + instrumental, za (behind) + instrumental, mezi (between) + instrumental.

examples.en: The book is on the table.
The table is in the kitchen.
The lamp is next to the bed.

rules.rule_name: Renting and buying — pronajmout si, koupit si
rules.pattern: pronajmout si (rent for oneself) + accusative, koupit si (buy for oneself) + accusative — reflexive possessive svůj for one's own
rules.explanation: When talking about renting or buying property, use the reflexive particle 'si' to indicate it's for yourself: pronajmout si byt (rent an apartment for oneself), koupit si dům (buy a house for oneself). The noun takes the accusative case. Related vocabulary: nájem (rent, the cost), nájemník (tenant), majitel (owner), pronajmout (to rent out), splácet hypotéku (pay off a mortgage). Common phrases: Hledám byt k pronájmu (I'm looking for an apartment to rent), Kolik stojí nájem? (How much is the rent?), Byt má dvě místnosti (The apartment has two rooms).

examples.cz: Pronajmul jsem si byt v Praze.
examples.en: I rented an apartment in Prague.
I want to buy a house.
I'm looking for an apartment to rent.

rules.rule_name: Motion verbs — jít vs jet vs chodit vs jezdit
rules.pattern: jít/jet = one-time motion (on foot/by vehicle), chodit/jezdit = habitual/repeated motion
rules.explanation: Czech has two pairs of basic motion verbs: (1) jít (to go on foot, one time) vs chodit (to go on foot, habitually/repeatedly), (2) jet (to go by vehicle, one time) vs jezdit (to go by vehicle, habitually/repeatedly). This distinction is crucial: 'Jdu do práce' = I'm going to work (right now, on foot), but 'Chodím do práce' = I go to work (every day, habitually). Similarly: 'Jedu do Prahy' = I'm going to Prague (by train/car now), 'Ježdím do Prahy' = I go to Prague regularly. Other motion verbs: běžet (run, one-time), běhat (run, habitual), letět (fly, one-time), létat (fly, habitual), plavat (swim), lézt (crawl).

examples.en: I'm going to the store. (right now, on foot)
I go to school every day. (habitual)
I'm taking the train to Brno. (right now)
We go to the cottage on weekends. (habitual)

rules.rule_name: Prefixed motion verbs — přijít, odejít, dojít, vyjít, projít
rules.pattern: prefix + jít/jet = directional specification of motion
rules.explanation: Czech creates directional motion verbs by adding prefixes to jít (go on foot) and jet (go by vehicle). Common prefixes: při- (arrive: přijít/přijet), od- (leave: odejít/odjet), do- (reach destination: dojít/dojet), vy- (exit/go out: vyjít/vyjet), pro- (pass through: projít/projet), na- (climb onto: najít/najet), z- (descend: zajít/zajet), v- (enter: vejít/vjet). These prefixed forms are typically perfective (completed action). To say 'return': vracet se (imperfective), vrátit se (perfective). Note: najít (with na- prefix on jít) actually means 'to find', not 'to climb onto' — this is an irregular prefixed form.

examples.en: He arrived home at five o'clock.
They left the cinema after the film.

rules.pattern: Imperfective (process) ↔ Perfective (result) — paired by prefixes: u-, na-, s-/z-, vy-, do-, při-, od-
rules.explanation: Czech verbs come in aspect pairs: imperfective (ongoing/process) and perfective (completed/result). The perfective is usually formed by adding a prefix to the imperfective. Common prefix patterns: u- (dělat→udělat, vařit→uvařit), na- (psát→napsat, číst→přečíst — note 'na' for writing, 'pře' for reading), s-/z- (dělat→zdělat — rare; more commonly: tvořit→stvořit), vy- (myt→vymýt, brát→vzít), do- (dělat→dodělat, číst→dočíst), při- (nést→přinést), od- (nést→odnést). Not every prefix works with every verb — the pairs must be learned. The key semantic distinction: imperfective answers 'What were you doing?' (psal jsem = I was writing), perfective answers 'What did you do?' (napsal jsem = I wrote [and finished]).

examples.en: to do — process → to do — completed
to write — process → to write — completed
to read — process → to read — completed
to buy — process → to buy — completed

rules.rule_name: Direction adverbs — kam, odkud, tudy, tam, sem, ven
rules.pattern: kam (where to), odkud (where from), tudy (this way), tam (there), sem (here/to here), ven (outside/out)
rules.explanation: Czech direction adverbs specify movement toward or away. kam = where to? (asks about destination: Kam jdeš? = Where are you going?), odkud = where from? (Odkud jsi? = Where are you from?), tudy = this way/through here (Tudy se jde k nádraží = This way to the station), tam = there (Jdu tam = I'm going there), sem = here/to here (Pojď sem = Come here), ven = outside/out (Jdu ven = I'm going outside), dovnitř = inside/in (Jdu dovnitř = I'm going inside), nahoru = upward (Jdu nahoru = I'm going up), dolů = downward (Jdu dolů = I'm going down). These pair with motion verbs to give precise directional information.

examples.en: Where are you going? — I'm going to school.

rules.rule_name: Aspect in context — when to use imperfective vs perfective
rules.pattern: Imperfective for ongoing/repeated/habitual; perfective for single completed actions with results
rules.explanation: The choice between imperfective and perfective depends on what you want to communicate. USE IMPERFECTIVE when: describing a process (psal jsem celý večer = I was writing all evening), habitual actions (každý den čtu = I read every day), simultaneous actions (když jsem vařil, ona se dívala = while I was cooking, she was watching), asking 'what were you doing?'. USE PERFECTIVE when: describing a completed action with a result (napsal jsem e-mail = I wrote an email [it's done]), single events (včera jsem koupil auto = yesterday I bought a car), sequential actions (přišel, sedl si a začal číst = he came, sat down, and started reading), asking 'what did you do?'. At A2 level, learners need to RECOGNIZE the distinction — active production is B1.

examples.en: I was writing a letter when she called. (imperfective — process)

examples.cz: Napsal jsem dopis a odeslal ho.
examples.en: I wrote a letter and sent it. (perfective — completed)
Every day I cook lunch. (imperfective — habitual)
Yesterday I cooked svíčková. (perfective — single completed)

rules.rule_name: A1 grammar summary — case, tense, and agreement overview
rules.pattern: Consolidation: nominative + accusative, present tense, gender agreement, pronouns, basic word order
rules.explanation: A1 LEVEL SUMMARY: (1) CASES: You know nominative (subject/dictionary form) and accusative (direct object: fem -a→-u, masc animate changes, masc inanimate and neuter unchanged). (2) TENSES: Present tense only — být (jsem, jsi, je, jsme, jste, jsou), mít (mám, máš, má, máme, máte, mají), regular verbs by conjugation class (-á, -í, -e). (3) GENDER: Three genders (masculine animate/inanimate, feminine, neuter) determine adjective endings (-ý, -á, -é) and pronoun forms. (4) PRONOUNS: Personal (já, ty, on/ona/ono, my, vy, oni/ony), possessive (můj, tvůj, jeho, její, náš, váš, jejich). (5) WORD ORDER: SVO default, flexible, verb in second position for clitics (se, si, jsem). (6) NEGATION: ne- prefix on verbs. (7) QUESTIONS: intonation or question words (kdo, co, kde, kam, kdy, jak, proč).

examples.cz: Jsem student a bydlím v Praze.
examples.en: I am a student and I live in Prague.
I have a new book. (accusative: nová→novou, kniha→knihu)
I don't understand, can you repeat that?

rules.rule_name: A2 grammar summary — complete case system, aspect, tenses, complex sentences
rules.pattern: Consolidation: all 6 cases, present/past/future/conditional, aspect recognition, adjective comparison, complex sentences
rules.explanation: A2 LEVEL SUMMARY: (1) CASES: All 6 cases — nominative (1st, subject), genitive (2nd, possession/quantity/negation), dative (3rd, indirect object/experiencer), accusative (4th, direct object), locative (5th, after v/na/o/po), instrumental (6th, means/accompaniment/profession). (2) TENSES: Present, past (l-participle + jsem/jsi), future (budu + infinitive or perfective present), conditional (bych + l-participle). (3) ASPECT: Recognize imperfective (process) vs perfective (completion) pairs — active production is B1. (4) ADJECTIVES: Comparison (-ejší/-ější, irregular: lepší, větší), superlative (nej-). (5) MODALS: muset (must), moct (can), smět (may), chtít (want). (6) SENTENCES: Compound (a, ale, nebo, proto, takže), complex (že, protože, když, aby). (7) CLITICS: Second position (jsem, se, si, by). (8) MOTION VERBS: jít/jet (one-time), chodit/jezdit (habitual), prefixed forms (přijít, odejít, vrátit se).

examples.cz: Kdybych měl čas, šel bych do kina.
examples.en: If I had time, I would go to the cinema. (conditional + past)

examples.cz: Včera jsem koupil knihu, kterou mi doporučil učitel.
examples.en: Yesterday I bought a book that the teacher recommended to me. (past + relative clause)

examples.cz: Musím jít k lékaři, protože mě bolí krk.
examples.en: I must go to the doctor because my throat hurts. (modal + dative + causal)

examples.cz: Přišel domů, svlékl si kabát a sedl si ke stolu.
examples.en: He came home, took off his coat, and sat down at the table. (sequential perfective past)
